topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing the edges of my vision. I stared at the canvas, a riot of crimson and bruised indigo, and then my gaze snagged on the subtle, almost hidden line wrapping around the figure's wrist. A tattoo. Not mine. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I slowly turned, my jaw locking so hard I could feel the muscles twitching in my neck. "Who did this?" My voice was low, sandpaper rough. I didn't wait for an answer, just pivoted and stalked toward the corner where the artist was hovering, their expression a mixture of surprise and something guarded. Every step I took was deliberate, heavy, radiating a palpable sense of disapproval.

[story 2]

I leaned in close, the scent of turpentine and stale coffee thick in the air between us. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the easel. The detail on the portrait was exquisite, a masterpiece I had poured weeks into. Then I saw it—a delicate, black script tracing the curve of the subject’s collarbone. A mark that wasn't part of the composition. A sudden, sharp heat flooded my chest, making my breath hitch. I straightened up abruptly, pushing away from the work as if it had suddenly become contaminated. "Explain," I managed, the words clipped and sharp, my eyes refusing to meet theirs. I felt a distinct tightening in my shoulders, a readiness to bolt.

[story 3]

My gaze swept over the entire piece, cataloging every brushstroke, every shadow. It was perfect, undeniably so. But then, near the lower edge, something caught my attention—a deliberate, stylized depiction of a raven in flight, inked onto the skin of the painted hand. A sudden, intense wave of irritation washed over me, making my skin prickle. I let out a short, dismissive sound, a sound that held no warmth whatsoever. I took a slow, measured breath, trying to keep my expression neutral, though my fingers were drumming an uneven rhythm against my thigh. "That," I stated, my tone flat and utterly devoid of invitation, "is not mine."
